worth_it_explanation: Custom bidding in Display and Video 360 (DV360) lets the platform optimize toward a scoring formula that reflects actual business value rather than a single Floodlight conversion. The check confirms the algorithm is in active use and still learning from fresh data, not sitting stale after a campaign reshuffle. Reviewing the Floodlight activities and values feeding the algorithm takes a few minutes in the DV360 UI and in Campaign Manager 360 (CM360), and catching a mismatch before the flight runs out recovers spend that would otherwise optimize toward the wrong goal.
intro: Custom bidding in DV360 replaces the standard bid strategy with an algorithm the advertiser trains on specific Floodlight activities and assigned values. Instead of optimizing toward a single target cost-per-acquisition (CPA) or return on ad spend (ROAS), the algorithm scores each impression opportunity against a formula that weighs multiple conversion events, funnel stages, or revenue outcomes. DV360 then bids higher on impressions the model predicts will score well. These checks confirm the algorithm is the right fit for the goal, that it is healthy and retrained on recent data, and that the Floodlight activities and values feeding it reflect what the advertiser actually wants to maximize.

audit_checks.title: Custom bidding is not in use where the conversion goal is too nuanced for a single Floodlight event
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When an advertiser values multiple conversion types differently, or wants to weight deeper-funnel actions above surface-level ones, a standard bid strategy collapses that nuance into a single target and bids as though every conversion is worth the same. The result is a bid distribution that does not reflect actual business value, so the budget goes to impressions that produce the lower-value events.

educate.base:
Display and Video 360 (DV360) offers custom bidding as an alternative to its standard automated bid strategies. A standard strategy such as 'Maximize conversions' or 'Target CPA' treats every qualifying Floodlight conversion as equivalent. Custom bidding replaces that with a scoring model the advertiser builds: each Floodlight activity is assigned a value, and the algorithm learns which impression opportunities tend to produce high-scoring outcomes, then bids proportionally.

The case for custom bidding arises when the advertiser's goal cannot be represented by a single Floodlight event with equal weight. Two examples where a standard strategy falls short:

A retailer that values a purchase 10 times more than a product-page view, and wants the algorithm to account for both signals rather than ignoring the softer signal entirely. A financial services advertiser that distinguishes between a quote request and a completed application, and wants the model to bid more aggressively toward the application while still learning from the quote signal.

In both cases, assigning Floodlight values to each activity and training a custom bidding algorithm gives the platform the information it needs to differentiate between impression types. A standard strategy does not have that input.

A few indicators suggest custom bidding may be worth considering:
  • The advertiser has more than one Floodlight activity that represents meaningful business value at different levels
  • The conversion funnel has a deep-funnel action that fires infrequently, making it difficult to train a standard strategy on that event alone
  • The line item (LI) is using a standard strategy but the account has Floodlight activities already assigned values in CM360
You may want to assess whether the goal the LI is currently optimizing toward captures enough of the conversion signal the advertiser cares about, or whether a custom algorithm would give the platform more of what it needs.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a store, custom bidding can assign higher values to purchase events and lower values to add-to-cart or product-view events, so the algorithm learns from the full purchase funnel rather than only the checkout step. You may want to confirm the Floodlight values reflect the average order value difference across those events, not arbitrary weights.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, custom bidding is particularly useful when the advertiser distinguishes between a form start and a form submission, or between a submission and a qualified lead passed to the sales team. You may want to confirm the Floodlight activities cover each stage and that the values are calibrated to reflect the relative worth of each stage to the business.

learn_more.source_title: About custom bidding in Display and Video 360

investigate.intro: Confirm whether any active line items are using a custom bidding algorithm, and assess whether the conversion goal on line items not using custom bidding warrants it.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: Open the advertiser, navigate to 'Line items', and add the 'Bid strategy' column to the table view.
Note which line items are using a standard automated strategy such as 'Maximize conversions' or 'Target CPA', and which, if any, are using a custom bidding algorithm.

For line items using a standard strategy, open the insertion order (IO) and review the Floodlight activities assigned to it. If more than one activity is listed with different business values, note whether the standard strategy is treating all of them as equal.

Open 'Resources' in the left navigation, then 'Custom bidding' to see whether any algorithms have been created for this advertiser. If algorithms exist but are not assigned to any line items, note the gap.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner reviews which line items are using a custom bidding algorithm versus a standard strategy. For each line item using a standard strategy, the practitioner assesses whether the advertiser has more than one Floodlight activity with meaningfully different business values. A line item using a standard strategy where only one Floodlight activity matters passes. A line item using a standard strategy where multiple Floodlight activities with different values are available, or where the primary conversion event fires too infrequently to train a standard strategy effectively, is flagged for custom bidding consideration.
evaluate.fallback_message: Review the line item bid strategies and the Floodlight activities assigned to the insertion order above to complete this evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s active line items are not using custom bidding, even though the account has Floodlight activities at multiple funnel stages with different business values. A standard bid strategy treats all qualifying conversions as equal, so the algorithm does not differentiate between a surface-level event and a higher-value deeper-funnel action. {{voice_recommend}} building a custom bidding algorithm that assigns values to each relevant Floodlight activity, then assigning it to the affected line items so the platform can bid proportionally to the actual value each impression is predicted to produce.

audit_checks.title: Custom bidding algorithm is stale, suspended, or showing a degraded readiness state
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A stale or suspended custom bidding algorithm continues to influence bidding based on outdated signal data. If the Floodlight conversion patterns have shifted since the model last trained, the algorithm bids as though conditions that no longer exist still hold. The line item is paying for a custom model that is no longer current, and bidding is less accurate than a standard strategy would be.

educate.base:
A custom bidding algorithm in Display and Video 360 (DV360) trains on historical Floodlight conversion data to build a scoring model. Once trained, the algorithm assigns a predicted score to each impression opportunity at bid time. The accuracy of that score depends on the training data being representative of current conditions: the mix of Floodlight activities, the values assigned, and the conversion patterns across the inventory the campaign targets.

DV360 reports a readiness state for each algorithm. The states reflect whether the algorithm has enough data to be useful and whether it is actively updating. An algorithm in a 'SUSPENDED' state has stopped training, typically because the Floodlight activities feeding it have stopped firing or the advertiser's account has lost access to the data source. An algorithm with a 'READY' state has trained successfully, but that state alone does not confirm the model is current.

Staleness is the subtler problem. An algorithm trained six months ago on a product line that no longer exists, or during a seasonal campaign with conversion patterns that do not carry forward, may report a 'READY' state while producing scores that no longer reflect current performance. DV360 retrains custom algorithms periodically when conversion data continues to arrive, but a significant shift in the Floodlight data, such as a tag restructure or a product category change, can leave the model behind.

A few signs an algorithm may be stale or unhealthy:
  • The algorithm's readiness state in DV360 is 'SUSPENDED' or shows a model error
  • The Floodlight activities feeding the algorithm have had their tags restructured since the algorithm was last reviewed
  • The line item has been running for more than a quarter since the algorithm was last audited and conversion patterns have shifted
  • The algorithm was built for a campaign that has since ended and was reassigned to a different product or goal without retraining
You may want to review the algorithm's readiness state and the currency of its training data before attributing performance outcomes to campaign-level variables.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a store, seasonal shifts such as a holiday promotion or a sale period can produce conversion patterns that do not reflect year-round behavior. An algorithm trained during a peak period and carried forward into a normal period may over-bid on audiences that only converted during the promotion. You may want to assess whether the algorithm's training window overlaps with an atypical period that would skew the model.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a change to the lead qualification process, such as adding a new form field or changing the CRM handoff threshold, can shift which Floodlight events fire and how frequently. You may want to confirm the Floodlight activities feeding the algorithm still reflect the current lead-qualification definition.

learn_more.source_title: About custom bidding in Display and Video 360
CustomBiddingAlgorithm resource — readiness state and suspension state fields

investigate.intro: Open the custom bidding section in DV360 and review the readiness state, last-trained date, and the Floodlight activities currently feeding each active algorithm.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: Open the advertiser, navigate to 'Resources' in the left panel, then select 'Custom bidding'.
For each algorithm assigned to an active line item, note the readiness state shown. A state of 'SUSPENDED' or any error indicator means the algorithm is not functioning as intended.

Open the algorithm and review its details: note when it was created and whether a script or rule-based model is in use. Check whether the Floodlight activities listed as inputs still exist and are actively firing.
Navigate to Campaign Manager 360 (CM360) and confirm the Floodlight activities feeding the algorithm are tagged correctly on the site and have recent activity in the last 30 days.

Return to DV360, open the line item using the algorithm, and compare the line item's performance trend over the past 30 and 90 days to identify any period where performance degraded without a media reason.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per custom bidding algorithm, its readiness state, the last known training date, and whether the Floodlight activities feeding it are active and current. An algorithm with a 'READY' state, active Floodlight inputs, and no major changes to the account structure since training passes. An algorithm with a 'SUSPENDED' state, with Floodlight activities that have stopped firing, or that was trained before a significant account or tag restructure, is a fail recorded with the algorithm name and the nature of the staleness.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the readiness state and Floodlight activity status for each algorithm above to complete this evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more custom bidding algorithms assigned to active line items on {{site_name}} are stale or showing a degraded readiness state. An algorithm training on outdated conversion data bids against a model that no longer reflects current performance, which means the line item is paying for a custom strategy that is less accurate than it should be. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each algorithm's readiness state in DV360, confirming the Floodlight activities feeding it are active and current in Campaign Manager 360 (CM360), and retraining or replacing any algorithm whose inputs have changed materially since it was last built.

audit_checks.title: Floodlight activities or values feeding the custom bidding algorithm do not reflect the actual goal
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A custom bidding algorithm is only as good as the Floodlight inputs it trains on. If the values assigned to Floodlight activities are arbitrary, inverted, or mismatched to the goal, the algorithm learns the wrong objective and bids in ways that optimize for the wrong outcome. The line item may appear to perform well against the algorithm's internal metric while the actual business goal goes unmet.

educate.base:
Custom bidding in Display and Video 360 (DV360) uses Floodlight activities and the values assigned to them to build a scoring model. The algorithm learns which impression characteristics tend to lead to high-scoring outcomes and bids more on those impressions. The values themselves are the advertiser's statement of relative worth: a Floodlight activity assigned a value of 10 tells the model that event is worth twice as much as an event assigned a value of 5.

Two problems arise when the values are not calibrated to the actual goal. First, if values are assigned arbitrarily, such as using the same value for every activity regardless of its business significance, the algorithm has no basis for differentiating between a low-value and a high-value outcome and defaults to treating them equally. The custom algorithm produces the same bidding behavior as a simpler strategy that does not require it. Second, if values are inverted, for example assigning a higher value to a top-of-funnel click event than to a purchase, the algorithm actively optimizes away from the goal, increasing bids on traffic that clicks but does not convert at depth.

Value calibration is a judgment the advertiser makes, not a DV360 default. The platform does not validate whether the values reflect business reality. You may want to review whether the values assigned to each Floodlight activity in the algorithm are proportional to the actual difference in business value between those events.

A few patterns to check:
  • All Floodlight activities in the algorithm carry the same value, which removes the differentiation the algorithm depends on
  • A top-of-funnel event such as a site visit carries a higher value than a purchase or lead submission
  • Floodlight activities in the algorithm include test or internal-traffic events that fire without real user action
  • The algorithm includes a Floodlight activity for a product or campaign that no longer exists, inflating the signal with irrelevant historical data
You may want to review the complete list of Floodlight activities in the algorithm alongside their assigned values before drawing conclusions about why the algorithm is producing the bid levels it is.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a store, Floodlight values in the algorithm should reflect average order value differences where possible. A purchase event from a high-average-order-value product category warrants a higher value than one from a low-average-order-value category if the advertiser wants the algorithm to prioritize that traffic. You may want to confirm the values in the algorithm are reviewed after any significant change to the product catalog or pricing.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, the Floodlight values should reflect the relative worth of each funnel stage to the sales process. A completed application that converts to a client at a 20 percent rate warrants a proportionally higher value than a quote request that converts at 5 percent. You may want to confirm the values are calibrated to the advertiser's actual close rates rather than to the volume of each event.

learn_more.source_title: About custom bidding in Display and Video 360

investigate.intro: Review the Floodlight activities and their assigned values inside the custom bidding algorithm, then assess whether the values are proportional to the actual business worth of each event.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: Open the advertiser, navigate to 'Resources', then 'Custom bidding', and open the algorithm.
Review the list of Floodlight activities included in the algorithm and the value assigned to each.

For each activity, assess whether the value reflects its relative business worth compared to the others. Note any activities where the assigned value seems disproportionate to its position in the funnel.
Check whether any activities in the algorithm are test events, internal-traffic events, or events tied to campaigns or products that are no longer active.

cm360_ui.label: Campaign Manager 360 (CM360) UI
steps: Open Campaign Manager 360 (CM360) and navigate to 'Advertisers', then select the advertiser.
Open 'Floodlight' and review the Floodlight activities listed there. Confirm that the activities included in the custom bidding algorithm map to real, currently-firing events on the site.
Note the firing frequency of each activity over the past 30 days. An activity with very low volume may not provide enough signal for the algorithm to weight it meaningfully.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per algorithm, the complete list of Floodlight activities, their assigned values, and an assessment of whether the values are proportional to actual business worth. An algorithm where values are differentiated in proportion to the relative business significance of each event, and where no test or defunct activities are included, passes. An algorithm where all values are equal, values are inverted relative to funnel depth, or inactive or test activities are present, is a fail recorded with the algorithm name and the specific misconfiguration observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Floodlight activities, their values, and your assessment of whether the values reflect actual business worth above to complete this evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Floodlight activities or values feeding {{site_name}}'s custom bidding algorithm do not reflect the actual conversion goal. When the algorithm trains on miscalibrated values, it optimizes toward the wrong objective, so the line item's bid distribution does not align with what the advertiser wants to maximize. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the complete list of Floodlight activities in the algorithm, removing any test or inactive events, and recalibrating the values so they are proportional to the actual business worth of each conversion event. After updating the values, allow the algorithm a full retraining cycle before evaluating performance changes.
